repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
## Problem

`vim.Range` and `vim.Pos` have signature mismatches on the docs of some functions.

## Solution

Split the "module" functions from the "class" functions (just like it's done in other modules like `vim.version`) and regenerate the docs.

Addresses point 3 of https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/38561 (`Range.cursor signature mismatch with docs. The example won't work as written.`)

Note: just like with `vim.version()`, creating `vim.pos()` and `vim.range()` functions via metatables means that documentation for them is not automatically generated from function signatures. Is it a good option to hardcode it on the `@brief` section of `vim.range` and `vim.pos`, just like with `vim.range()`? (this is not done yet)

comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -188,29 +186,28 @@ function Range:is_empty()
   return cmp_pos(self.start_row, self.start_col, inclusive_end_row, inclusive_end_col) ~= -1
 end
 
---- Checks whether {outer} range contains {inner} range or position.
+--- Checks whether range contains {inner} range or position.
 ---
----@param outer vim.Range
 ---@param inner vim.Range|vim.Pos
----@return boolean `true` if {outer} range fully contains {inner} range or position.
-function Range.has(outer, inner)
-  if getmetatable(inner) == vim.pos then
+---@return boolean `true` if range fully contains {inner} range or position.
+function Range:has(inner)
+  if getmetatable(inner) == vim.pos._Pos then

comment_data.body: We could use a different check, like `inner[4] == nil` (or `inner[5] == nil` if/when a new field for `win` is added)
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -188,29 +186,28 @@ function Range:is_empty()
   return cmp_pos(self.start_row, self.start_col, inclusive_end_row, inclusive_end_col) ~= -1
 end
 
---- Checks whether {outer} range contains {inner} range or position.
+--- Checks whether range contains {inner} range or position.
 ---
----@param outer vim.Range
 ---@param inner vim.Range|vim.Pos
----@return boolean `true` if {outer} range fully contains {inner} range or position.
-function Range.has(outer, inner)
-  if getmetatable(inner) == vim.pos then
+---@return boolean `true` if range fully contains {inner} range or position.
+function Range:has(inner)
+  if getmetatable(inner) == vim.pos._Pos then

comment_data.body:
This pattern is common so not too concerned. Though maybe we could do the inverse to avoid exposing `vim._Pos` (i.e. check for Range's own metatable, if it has one).

Somewhat related: we discussed a `kind` field in https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/25509 et al.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -188,29 +186,28 @@ function Range:is_empty()
   return cmp_pos(self.start_row, self.start_col, inclusive_end_row, inclusive_end_col) ~= -1
 end
 
---- Checks whether {outer} range contains {inner} range or position.
+--- Checks whether range contains {inner} range or position.
 ---
----@param outer vim.Range
 ---@param inner vim.Range|vim.Pos
----@return boolean `true` if {outer} range fully contains {inner} range or position.
-function Range.has(outer, inner)
-  if getmetatable(inner) == vim.pos then
+---@return boolean `true` if range fully contains {inner} range or position.
+function Range:has(inner)
+  if getmetatable(inner) == vim.pos._Pos then

comment_regular_data.body:
> Note: just like with `vim.version()`, creating `vim.pos()` and `vim.range()` functions via metatables means that documentation for them is not automatically generated from function signatures. Is it a good option to hardcode it on the `@brief` section of `vim.range` and `vim.pos`, just like with `vim.range()`? (this is not done yet)

umm let's do whatever vim.version does. but in a separate PR

I just noticed this change, but when I created `pos.lua`, my intention was that we didn't need to distinguish between methods and functions this way. Before this PR, I defined `Pos.extmark(pos)` instead of `Pos:extmark()` because it was meant to be callable as `vim.pos.extmark(pos)`, not just as `pos:extmark()`. This is consistent with Lua itself; for example, with `string`, `format` can be called in two ways: `string.format("%s", pattern)` and `("%s"):format(pattern)`. Therefore, the "incorrect" here was intentional.

The real issue should lie within the documentation generation script. We should make it so that functions defined without `:`, like `Pos.extmark(pos)`, are generated as `vim.pos.extmark(pos)`, rather than `Pos:extmark(pos)`, while still preserving other functions defined using the `:` syntax.

In your particular example, I don't think I would ever call `pos:extmark()` to create a new position. For me, it makes sense to call `vim.pos.extmark()` instead. But, yes, previously you were able to either call `vim.pos.to_extmark()` and provide a `Pos` as the first argument while now you **have** to use `Pos:to_extmark()`.

I could revert this change and try to modify the script used to generate documentation instead if that's deemed desirable.

https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/9c5fba5df0b60cd25ac2c180a7d82fca47a105e6/src/gen/luacats_parser.lua#L314

Seems to intentionally include both functions declared with `.` and `:` as classes when they have a `---@class` annotation . So, I would have to test if this new approach results in some other part of the documentation being generated in an undesirable way.

> I defined `Pos.extmark(pos)` instead of `Pos:extmark()` because it was meant to be callable as `vim.pos.extmark(pos)`, not just as `pos:extmark()`

Do you mean `to_extmark()`? 

With `x:foo()` notation, it is still possible to do `x.foo(x)`. But I see that after c530fd8e7526, there are separate `M` and `Pos` modules instead of just one `Pos` module. Was that only for the doc generator @TheLeoP , or was the functionality change intended?

> With x:foo() notation, it is still possible to do x.foo(x). But I see that after https://github.com/neovim/neovim/commit/c530fd8e752607f57b83854b74b029eabe8d57c6, there are separate M and Pos modules instead of just one Pos module. Was that only for the doc generator, or was the functionality change intended?

The change was motivated by the doc generator, but it was also intended. In my mind, it makes sense that functions to create `Pos` objects are accessed through `vim.pos.` and functions to transform `Pos` objects to other representations are accessed through `Pos:`. But, I do see the value in also supporting `vim.pos` for `Pos` transformations.
I think if we can fix the doc generation, and if there are no downsides I'm forgetting, having one module that supports both ways seems like a "nice to have" and also simplifies the module a bit.

Got it. What would be the ideal output of the doc generation for cases like this one?

Two tags with the same description:

```vimdoc
Pos:to_extmark()                          *vim.pos.extkmark()* *Pos:to_extmark()*
    Converts |vim.Pos| to extmark position (see |api-indexing|).

    Return (multiple): ~
        (`integer`)
        (`integer`)
```

Two tags, each with a description:

```vimdoc
Pos:to_extmark()                                            *Pos:to_extmark()*
    Converts |vim.Pos| to extmark position (see |api-indexing|).

    Return (multiple): ~
        (`integer`)
        (`integer`)

vim.pos.to_extmark({pos})                                            *vim.pos.extkmark()*
    Converts |vim.Pos| to extmark position (see |api-indexing|).

    Return (multiple): ~
        (`integer`)
        (`integer`)
```

> Two tags with the same description:

Yeah that's a good approach IMO.

title: docs: display `vim.range` and `vim.pos` module functions correctly
